---
title: "Week 5: Research Questions and Ethics"
subtitle: "MC 501 Research Methods in Mass Media"
date: "2026-09-22"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
---

## This Week

This week you move from broad curiosity to precise questioning. Chapter 7 teaches you how to construct research questions that are specific, measurable, and grounded in theory. Chapter 8 addresses the ethical obligations that come with studying human communication --- from informed consent to the responsibilities of working with publicly available data.

**Reading:** Chapter 7 (The Research Question) and Chapter 8 (The Ethics of Inquiry)

---

## What You Need to Do This Week

### 1. Read Chapters 7 and 8

As you read, pay attention to:

- The difference between a topic and a research question
- How variables emerge naturally from well-constructed RQs
- Why ethical considerations apply even when analyzing public social media posts
- The distinction between exempt, expedited, and full IRB review

### 2. Select Three Peer-Reviewed Articles for Annotation

MC 451 students annotate one article this week. MC 501 requires three. Choose all three articles before you begin annotating. Each must be peer-reviewed, from a reputable communication or media journal, and published within the last 10 years (2016 or later). All three must be directly relevant to your proposed research topic.

### 3. Complete the Annotated Manuscripts Assignment (below)

---

## Assignment: Annotated Manuscripts (25 pts)

**Due:** Friday 11:59 PM | **Submit to:** Blackboard (PDF uploads --- 3 files)

### The Mission

Reading like a researcher means looking past the story and identifying the underlying mechanics. Every published study is built on a scaffold of variables, theories, and methodological choices --- and learning to see that scaffold is a skill you will use in every assignment that follows. This week you will use Zotero to dissect three peer-reviewed articles, annotating the structural elements that will inform your own research design. The goal is not to summarize but to reverse-engineer: identify what the authors measured, what theory guided their predictions, and how their choices connect to your own project.

### Step 1: Selection

For each of the three articles:

1. Choose a peer-reviewed journal article directly relevant to your proposed research topic
2. Add it to your Zotero library using the browser connector or by dragging the PDF into Zotero
3. Each article must meet all three criteria:
   - From a reputable communication or media journal (e.g., *Journal of Communication*, *Communication Research*, *Journalism & Mass Communication Quarterly*, *New Media & Society*, *Human Communication Research*)
   - Peer-reviewed (not a book review, editorial, or conference abstract)
   - Published within the last 10 years (2016 or later)

The three articles should represent different angles on your topic --- different methods, different theoretical lenses, or different media contexts. Avoid selecting three articles that say essentially the same thing.

### Step 2: Deep Dissection (repeat for each article)

Open each PDF in the Zotero built-in reader and annotate it thoroughly:

1. **Highlight variables** --- use one consistent color to highlight every mention of independent and dependent variables. Look in the literature review, hypotheses, and methods sections.
2. **Add margin notes** --- write at least 5 margin notes per article explaining how specific passages connect to your own project. These should be analytical connections, not summaries.
3. **Identify the primary theory** --- find and tag the main theoretical framework. Add a note at the first mention explaining what the theory predicts and why the authors chose it.

### Step 3: Export

For each article:

1. In Zotero, right-click the PDF attachment and select **Export PDF**
2. Ensure **Include Annotations** is checked
3. Save the exported PDF to your `02_Literature` folder in your Methods Vault

### Submission

Upload all three annotated PDFs to Blackboard.

**File names:** `Lastname_AnnotatedManuscript_1.pdf`, `Lastname_AnnotatedManuscript_2.pdf`, `Lastname_AnnotatedManuscript_3.pdf`

### Grading Criteria

| Criterion | Points |
|-----------|--------|
| All 3 articles are peer-reviewed and relevant to the proposed topic | 5 |
| Variables highlighted consistently in each article | 5 |
| At least 5 substantive margin notes per article (15 total) | 5 |
| Primary theory identified and explained in each article | 5 |
| Files exported with annotations and named correctly | 5 |
| **Total** | **25** |

---

## Connecting Forward

Next week you will read Chapters 9 and 10 --- the methods overview and qualitative methods. You will also draft your Research Proposal, a structured sentence outline that forces the specificity a one-page prospectus cannot achieve. The variables, theories, and methods you identified across all three annotations will feed directly into that proposal.

---

## Weekly Journal (10 pts)

Your journal entry for this week is included in this download as `journal_week_05.qmd`. Open it in Obsidian, choose your reflection approach (Connector, Troubleshooter, or Critic), and write 450--500 words. Your entry must engage with the graduate extension reading for this week --- surface-level summaries of the main chapters will not receive full credit.

**Due:** Friday 11:59 PM | **Submit:** Push to GitHub via GitHub Desktop, then submit rendered PDF to Blackboard.

---

## Getting Help

- **Course website:** [Open Methods Hub](https://sim-lab-siue.github.io/liaison-program/) --- check here first
- **Email:** aleith@siue.edu (allow 24 hours Mon--Fri)
- **Office hours:** MW 10:00--11:30 AM and 1:30--3:00 PM, Dunham Hall 1017
- **Discord:** APLeith
